planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped camellias in fall in zones 7–10; fall planting allows root establishment before the winter bloom season. Set the root ball slightly above grade to ensure excellent drainage — camellias planted too deep quickly decline.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade or filtered sun; camellias are quintessential Gulf Coast and Southeast garden shrubs prized for their large, rose-like flowers in shades of white, pink, red, and bicolor that bloom from fall through early spring — exactly when little else flowers. Protect from harsh afternoon sun and desiccating winter winds, especially newly planted specimens. Apply a 2–3 inch layer of pine bark or pine straw mulch, keeping it away from the trunk. Fertilize with acid-forming camellia fertilizer after flowering ends in spring. Scale and petal blight are the most common issues; treat scale with horticultural oil and improve air circulation to reduce blight. Hundreds of named cultivars exist — choose bloom time (fall, winter, or spring-flowering) based on your frost window.

planting.note: Plant in fall in zones 8–10 for best establishment before bloom; in zones 7–8 spring planting after bloom is also effective. Set root ball 1–2 inches above the surrounding soil grade and backfill with native soil amended with pine bark.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–5.5) soil amended with pine bark in partial shade to morning sun with afternoon shade; Southern Indica azaleas are the classic massive-flowering shrubs of Deep South gardens, producing spectacular displays of white, pink, red, lavender, or salmon flowers in spring that completely cover the plant. These are large, fast-growing plants — give them full mature-size spacing rather than planting them too close. They are much more heat- and root-rot tolerant than Northern azaleas and are specifically bred for Gulf Coast conditions. Mulch deeply with pine straw to maintain moisture and soil acidity. Prune immediately after flowering ends in spring, as next year's buds set on summer growth. Lace bugs are the primary pest — treat with systemic insecticide in late spring.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, amended soil; in zones 8–10, fall planting is preferred. Dig a wide, shallow hole — 2x the width of the root ball — and set the plant at the same depth it grew in the container.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in morning sun with afternoon shade; bigleaf hydrangeas are the classic summer-blooming shrubs with large mophead or lacecap flower clusters in blue, pink, or white. Flower color depends on soil pH — blue in acidic soil (pH below 5.5) and pink in alkaline soil (pH above 6.5); to maintain blue, add aluminum sulfate and maintain pH below 6.0. In the South (zones 8–9) afternoon shade is essential to prevent wilting and maintain flower quality. Old-wood bloomers (most classic varieties) should be pruned only immediately after flowering — never in fall, as this removes next year's buds. Reblooming types such as 'Endless Summer' bloom on both old and new wood, making them more reliable after hard winters.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after soil has warmed to at least 65°F; space clumps 36–48 inches apart. In zone 8, mulch heavily in winter to protect the rhizome from killing frosts.

directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; shell ginger is a stunning tropical-looking perennial for Gulf Coast gardens, growing rapidly to 6–10 feet with broad, fragrant, cane-like stems and large lance-shaped leaves. In late spring and summer, mature canes produce cascading clusters of porcelain-white flowers with yellow and pink markings that resemble shells before opening — extraordinarily fragrant and beautiful. Only second-year canes flower; cut spent canes to the ground after bloom to make room for new growth. The variegated form ('Variegata') has gold-striped foliage and is slightly smaller. Plants freeze to the ground in zone 8 frosts but re-emerge from hardy rhizomes in spring with deep mulch protection.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; in zones 8a–8b, choose a south-facing wall or protected microclimate to extend the frost-free period and improve cold hardiness.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy loam or loamy soil with a pH of 6.0–7.0 in full sun; satsumas are the most cold-hardy commercially grown citrus, tolerating brief drops to 15–20°F on established trees, making them the premier citrus for Gulf Coast home gardens from Houston to Pensacola. The seedless, easily peeled fruits ripen from October through December with a sweet, rich flavor superior to most supermarket citrus. Grafted trees on trifoliate orange rootstock provide the best cold hardiness and smaller tree size. Fertilize with a citrus-specific fertilizer three times per year (spring, early summer, late summer). Protect young trees from freezes with frost cloth or lights; established trees in zone 8b rarely need protection. 'Owari' and 'Kimbrough' are the most cold-hardy cultivars.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown shrubs in spring; most elderberry varieties require cross-pollination for heavy fruiting — plant at least two different varieties 60 feet or less apart. Space plants 6–10 feet apart for a productive hedge.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; American elderberry is a fast-growing native shrub that produces massive flat-topped clusters of creamy white flowers in early summer — outstanding for pollinators — followed by heavy drooping clusters of small, dark purple-black berries in late summer. Berries are used for elderberry syrup, wine, jam, and juice; raw berries and all other plant parts are mildly toxic and should not be eaten uncooked. Prune out two-year-old canes after harvest to maintain productivity, as elderberries fruit on second-year wood. The plants sucker freely and can become large thickets — mow around the perimeter to control spread. 'Adams', 'Nova', and 'Bob Gordon' are reliably productive cultivars; for ornamental value, the dark-leaved 'Black Lace' is also fruiting.

planting.note: Surface-sow seed (do not cover — seed needs light to germinate) indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; seed requires light and warmth to germinate. Transplant to the garden after frost danger passes, placing in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun near water features, rain gardens, or boggy areas; cardinal flower produces brilliant scarlet flower spikes from midsummer into fall that are the premier nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds in eastern North America. The intensity of the red is almost unmatched in the native plant palette. Plants are short-lived perennials that self-seed freely — allow some seedlings to remain to perpetuate the colony, as individual plants typically live only 2–3 years. Division of offsets (basal rosettes) in early fall also maintains the planting. Plants tolerate standing water and thrive along stream banks and pond edges. The great blue lobelia (Lobelia siphilitica) is a closely related blue-flowered species equally valuable for pollinators.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost. Transplants set in spring establish quickly and may bloom their first summer.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; bee balm is a native meadow herb producing ragged, shaggy scarlet flower heads from June through August that attract hummingbirds, bumblebees, and numerous butterfly species. The square stems and aromatic leaves have a strong oregano-citrus scent; dried leaves make a pleasant herbal tea, and fresh leaves can season meats and salads. Powdery mildew is the primary problem — choose mildew-resistant cultivars ('Jacob Cline', 'Raspberry Wine', 'Grand Marshall') and ensure good air circulation. Divide every 2–3 years in spring to maintain vigor; mature clumps hollow out in the center and decline without regular division. Cut back by half in late May (Chelsea Chop) to delay bloom and reduce height.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; plants establish quickly and begin blooming the following spring. Direct seeding is possible but takes longer — divisions are the preferred propagation method.

directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia spiderwort is a native wildflower of open woods and roadsides producing three-petaled flowers in shades of blue-violet to purple, pink, or white that open in the morning and close by early afternoon. The strap-like, grass-like foliage is bold and attractive in the spring garden. After flowering, plants go semi-dormant in midsummer heat and may look tatty — cut back hard to 4–6 inches to encourage a flush of fresh foliage. Plants self-seed prolifically and spread by clump division; thin as needed. The closed flowers and young shoot tips are edible in salads, and the plant has a long history of use by eastern woodland peoples.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed for 60 days before sowing, or direct sow in fall and allow natural winter stratification; transplant in spring. Plants benefit from cool stratification and will not germinate reliably from unsratified seed.

directions: Plant in moist, average soil in full sun to partial shade; ironweed is a tall native perennial of moist meadows and streambanks producing intensely saturated royal-purple flower clusters in late summer and fall that are unmatched for pure color saturation in the late-season garden. The flowers are extraordinary butterfly magnets, particularly for monarchs, swallowtails, and skippers fueling up for fall migration. Ironweed is called 'iron' because the stems are so tough and fibrous that they were once used as tying material. To control height in garden settings, cut plants back by half in late June before flowering; this delays bloom slightly and reduces final height from 6–7 feet to 4–5 feet. Self-seeds moderately — deadhead after flowering if not needed.
